what_it_is: BioTRIZ technology-derived comparison matrix. Same six operational fields as the biology submatrix; cells encode engineering-domain solutions. Use as a baseline against biotriz_6x6_bio when comparing biological vs. engineering framings of the same contradiction.

when_to_use.ideal_user_profile: Biomimetics researchers, industrial designers seeking nature-inspired solutions, sustainability engineers, materials scientists exploring bio-inspired composites, architects interested in biological structural principles, and innovation consultants looking to break engineering fixation.
best_for: Finding nature-inspired solutions to engineering contradictions
Breaking design fixation by viewing problems through a biological lens
Substance-vs-Energy trade-offs (lightweight but strong, like bone or nacre)
Designing self-repairing, self-cleaning, or self-adapting systems
Exploring how biology handles Time-vs-Space trade-offs (growth, morphogenesis)
Generating sustainability-oriented concepts in early-stage design
Comparing biological vs. technological solution strategies for the same contradiction
not_suitable_for: Detailed engineering parameter trade-offs requiring 39-parameter specificity
Healthcare service delivery or governance problems
Problems where the solution must avoid biological metaphors (e.g., sterile environments)
when_to_use.prefer_over_alternatives_when: Use this matrix when seeking fundamentally different solution strategies by asking 'how does biology solve this?' Prefer it for early-stage conceptual design, biomimetic research, or when standard TRIZ solutions feel stale. The included matrix_technology data enables direct side-by-side comparison of how biology and technology resolve the same contradiction differently.

strengths.area: Nature-inspired problem solving at a high abstraction level
strengths.why: The 6 operational fields (Substance, Structure, Space, Time, Energy, Information) are derived from analysis of ~500 biological phenomena and ~2500 contradictions, providing a fundamentally different lens from engineering TRIZ. Biology solves problems using Information and Structure where technology uses Energy.

strengths.area: Revealing blind spots in conventional engineering thinking
strengths.why: Only 12% similarity with the technology-derived PRIZM matrix proves that biology and technology solve the same contradictions with radically different strategies, making this matrix ideal for breaking engineering fixation.

strengths.area: Rapid high-level ideation and conceptual exploration
strengths.why: The compact 6x6 format enables quick traversal of all 36 contradiction intersections, including self-contradictions (diagonal cells). Each cell contains 3-8 principles, providing rich but manageable suggestion sets.

Biological solutions inherently tend toward low energy, material efficiency, and closed-loop systems. Principles like Self-service (25), Dynamics (15), and Blessing in disguise (22) appear frequently, reflecting biology's preference for adaptive, waste-minimizing strategies.

weaknesses.why: Condensing 39 classical parameters into 6 fields sacrifices specificity. 'Energy' covers force, stress, temperature, power, noise, and harmful effects -- too broad for fine-grained engineering trade-offs.
The matrix lacks parameters for manufacturing precision, ease of repair, or production cost. It is concept-oriented, not process-oriented.
The biological analogies map poorly to purely digital or informational systems, despite the Information parameter.
